The user is asking if I can find some hidden connections in their notes. I suspect there's a typo and they meant "find/show." I’ll need to check the notes in their documents folder. I might be able to infer a network between software engineering and philosophy. It could help to compute backlinks and inspect links and tags, perhaps using ripgrep for wikilinks. Analyzing titles and content sounds essential for synthesizing evidence. I want to read indexes to understand the aggregation of themes better. So, let's look at some index files!

content.text:
---
id: Developer Experience
aliases:
  - DevEx
indexes: []
---

## Claim

Developer experience is the shape of the environment that determines how easily engineers can understand code, validate changes, and stay in productive flow.

## Why this note matters

- Developer velocity is not only about output metrics; it is also about the quality of the system engineers work inside.
- Good DevEx reduces wasted effort, context switching, and uncertainty.
- It gives a more actionable lens for improving engineering productivity than raw output numbers alone.

## Permanent notes from this topic

- [[Developer experience can be understood through feedback loops cognitive load and flow state]]
- [[Fast feedback loops are a core dimension of developer experience]]
- [[Cognitive load is a developer experience problem not just a code complexity problem]]
- [[Flow state depends on protecting uninterrupted focus and reducing reactive work]]

## Connections

- [[developer velocity]]
- [[Testing & Code Quality]]
- [[Technical Vision]]
- [[RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing]]
- [[RFC-01 - Directed, Bounded Frontend Architecture]]

## Practical questions

- How long does it take to validate a change locally and in CI?
- How much system context must an engineer hold to make one safe change?
- How often is focus broken by meetings, incidents, or unclear tasks?
- Which workflow frictions are harming confidence and speed the most?

## Related

- [[Maintainability means being able to change code confidently]]
- [[Feedback loops make change survivable]]

---
id: Philosophy
aliases: 
indexes:
---

---
id: JavaScript
aliases: []
indexes: []
---

## Claim

JavaScript is easiest to reason about when its behavior is organized around a few core ideas: values and coercion, lexical scope and closure, iteration and generators, asynchronous execution, and object delegation.

## Why this note matters

- JavaScript feels inconsistent when its features are learned as isolated tricks.
- Many surprising behaviors come from a small number of reusable models.
- This index ties together language fundamentals and execution behavior into one navigable map.

## Permanent notes from this topic

- [[Closure]]
- [[Iterators|Iterators turn stateful traversal into a standardized next-step protocol]]
- [[Generators let JavaScript pause and resume execution]]
- [[Asynchronous JavaScript]]
- [[Deep JavaScript]]

## Connections

- [[Event Loop]]
- [[Closure]]
- [[Iterators]]
- [[3 - Indexes/Generators]]
- [[3 - Indexes/Asynchronous JavaScript]]

## Practical questions

- Is this behavior explained by coercion, scope, or execution order?
- What determines which value a function can access?
- Is this control flow synchronous, iterated, paused, or resumed later?
- Is this object behavior using prototypes, `this`, or lexical capture?

## Related

- [[Event Loop Logic.canvas|Event Loop Logic]]

---
id: Testing & Code Quality
aliases: []
indexes:
  - '[[JavaScript]]'
---

## Claim

Maintainable frontend codebases depend on a system of testing, analysis, architecture, and delivery practices that make change safe.

## Why this note matters

- Testing alone is not enough to keep a UI codebase healthy.
- Confidence comes from patterns, processes, and systems working together.
- This topic maps directly to the RFC work on behaviour-focused testing and long-term frontend quality.

## Permanent notes from this topic

- [[Maintainability means being able to change code confidently]]
- [[Well-architected UI applications need patterns processes and systems]]
- [[Mocking code you own often signals poor separation of concerns]]
- [[Test types trade speed for realism and confidence]]

## Connections

- [[RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing]]
- [[RFC-01 - Directed, Bounded Frontend Architecture]]
- [[Technical Vision]]
- [[FE Design System Scaling Web Applications]]
- [[FE Design System API]]

## Practical questions

- Can we change behavior without fear of hidden regressions?
- Are business logic, data fetching, and UI concerns separated clearly enough to test well?
- Which quality checks run automatically before changes ship?
- Where is confidence coming from today: tests, conventions, or luck?

## Related

- [[Nx]]
- [[Web Performance]]

## Related

- [[3 - Indexes/Developer Experience]]
- [[Cognitive load is a developer experience problem not just a code complexity problem]]
- [[Fast feedback loops are a core dimension of developer experience]]

---
id: Designing Data-Intensive Applications
aliases:
  - DDIA
indexes: []
---

## Claim

Data systems work well when their guarantees, storage choices, and scaling strategies match the actual shape of load and failure in the system.

## Why this note matters

- DDIA explains distributed systems through tradeoffs instead of slogans.
- It gives vocabulary for reasoning about reliability, scale, storage, and consistency.
- It helps connect application simplicity to infrastructure guarantees.

## Permanent notes from this book

- [[Reliable systems tolerate faults instead of pretending faults disappear]]
- [[Scalability depends on explicit load and performance assumptions]]
- [[Data models should fit relationship shape and access patterns]]
- [[Storage engines are workload tradeoffs, not universal winners]]
- [[Replication and partitioning solve different scaling problems]]
- [[Transactions simplify applications by moving guarantees into the database]]

## Connections

- [[Software complexity comes from dependencies and obscurity]]
- [[Quality is a speed strategy, not a tradeoff]]
- [[FE Design System Scaling Web Applications]]
- [[FE Design System Real Time Updates]]
- [[RFC-03 - Frontend Error Handeling]]
- [[Technical Vision]]

## Practical questions

- What are the real load parameters of this system?
- Which guarantees belong in the application and which belong in the data layer?
- Are we choosing a storage model for the workload we actually have?
- What happens when one component fails but the system should keep serving users?
- Where are we accepting stale data, and is that tradeoff explicit?

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/Designing Data Intensive Applications The Big Ideas Behind Reliable, Scalable, and Maintainable Systems#^ddia-load-parameters]]

id: Web Performance
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Browser|Browser]]"
  - "[[JavaScript]]"
  - "[[Performance]]"
---

## Claim

Web performance matters because user perception, business outcomes, and technical metrics all interact, so teams need both the right metrics and the right measurement context.

## Why this note matters

- Performance is not only about raw speed; it is about what users feel and when they feel it.
- Different metrics answer different questions about loading, rendering, and interaction.
- Good performance work connects lab measurement, field data, and business impact.

## Permanent notes from this topic

- [[Web performance should be judged by user perception and business outcomes]]
- [[Web performance should be measured with field and lab data for different questions]]
- [[Percentiles reveal performance risk better than averages]]
- [[Performance goals depend on user context and the value being delivered]]
- [[Legacy load metrics are useful but incomplete]]
- [[Performance instrumentation should combine browser APIs vitals and RUM]]

## Connections

- [[Technical Vision]]
- [[RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing]]
- [[FE Design System Real Time Updates]]
- [[Scalability depends on explicit load and performance assumptions]]

## Practical questions

- Which moments matter most to users: initial load, interaction, or visual stability?
- Are we using lab data to improve changes and field data to understand real experience?
- Which percentile reflects the experience we actually want to protect?
- How do our business goals map to performance metrics?

## Related

- [[Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)]]
- [[Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)]]
- [[Interaction to Next Paint (INP)]]
- [[Time to first byte (TTFB)]]
- [[First Contentful Paint (FCP)]]
- [[Time to Interactive (TTI)]]

---
id: FE System Design
aliases: 
indexes:
---

---
id: Asynchronous JavaScript
aliases:
  - Aysynchronous JavaScript
indexes:
---

## Claim

Asynchronous JavaScript works by letting external work complete outside the call stack and then resuming JavaScript through queued callbacks and promise reactions.

## Why this note matters

- Async behavior only makes sense once the event loop, promise callbacks, and paused execution are connected.
- This topic explains why `fetch()` returns immediately, why promises resume later, and how `async`/`await` can be modeled conceptually.
- It also links language features like iterators and generators to how paused execution can be resumed.

## Permanent notes from this topic

- [[Async callbacks resume through different task queues depending on their source]]
- [[Promises let JavaScript resume work after external operations finish]]
- [[Async await can be modeled as generator execution resumed by promise resolution]]

## Connections

- [[3 - Indexes/Generators]]
- [[Iterators]]
- [[Closure]]
- [[JavaScript]]

## Practical questions

- What work is happening on the call stack versus outside JavaScript?
- Which queue will this callback run in?
- When does the current execution context pause and resume?
- Is this code waiting on a promise or blocking synchronously?

## Related

- [[Event Loop Logic.canvas|Event Loop Logic]]

---
id: A Philosophy of Software Design
aliases:
  - APOSD
indexes: []
---

## Claim

Good software design is mostly about reducing the cost of future change by minimizing [[Software complexity comes from dependencies and obscurity|dependencies and obscurity]].

## Why this note matters

- This book reframes design quality around maintainability, not cleverness.
- It gives a practical lens for evaluating code: how expensive is the next change?
- It connects architecture, APIs, error handling, comments, and naming under one idea: lower cognitive cost.

## Permanent notes from this book

- [[Software complexity comes from dependencies and obscurity]]
- [[Strategic programming treats design work as an investment]]
- [[Deep modules hide complexity behind simple interfaces]]
- [[Comments should capture non-obvious design knowledge]]

## Connections

- [[Testing & Code Quality]]
- [[developer velocity]]
- [[RFC-01 - Directed, Bounded Frontend Architecture]]
- [[RFC-03 - Frontend Error Handeling]]
- [[FE Design System Scaling Web Applications]]

## Practical questions

- Where is the change amplification in the system?
- Which APIs force callers to understand too much?
- Which abstractions hide useful detail versus important detail?
- Which comments explain intent that the code cannot express?
- What small design investment would make the next change cheaper?

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/A Philosophy of Software Design#^aposd-summary-principles]]

content.text:
--- id: developer velocity aliases: [] tags: [] --- ## Different measures for dev velocity - Lead time: Lead time measures the time from when a task or user story is added to the backlog until it is completed. Tracking lead time can help you understand how quickly the team is able to respond to new work and deliver value. - Code churn: Code churn is the number of lines of code added, modified, or deleted during a specific time period. High code churn can indicate issues with code quality, while low churn can signal stable and efficient development. Monitoring code churn can provide insights into the team’s development velocity and overall code quality. (GitHub) churn - Mean time to resolution (MTTR): MTTR measures the average time it takes for a team to resolve an issue, such as a bug or defect. A low MTTR can indicate a high development velocity and the ability to quickly address issues. ## Steps to improve - We can try to measure Lead time using Jira - Code churn...I am not sure, but maybe github has some tools? - We should gather feedback...ask devs where they feel their productivity is hindered. (I know we kinda do this on the retros, but I think it would be more valuable as a regular survey to have statistics so we can setup goals and ask more specific questions) ## Build a culture of Experimentation - We should strive to continually improve the process of achieving our goals - We should build the development department and the codebase to be easy to experiment on - We could do A/B testing on project & teams - Maybe we can provide time to experiment with new tools? Using "innovation days" ## DevEx ### 1. Feedback Loop: code review, automated tests, CI/CD - How to measure: code review turnaround time, Lowe bug reopen rates, high deployment frequency. ### 2. Cogitive Load: It refers to the mental effort, and processing capacity required by developers to code effectively - It means, establishing processes to minimize complex code structures, ensure comprehensive documentation of the code, safeguard them from timeline and delivery pressures, and reduce the context-switching instances that mostly result from multitasking. - We should try to continually improve the code complexity - Reading and code maintenance - testing - how does the complexity of code increases: Lack of documentation, architecture, over engineering, natural entropy of code - We can use cyclomatic complexity or McGave Complexity, halstead volume to measure complexity of the code - Maintainablity index: The Maintainability Index or MI is a score of how easy it is to maintain code. It is a combination of the four metrics: Cyclomatic complexity and Halstead volume, Lines of Code (LoC) and depth of inheritance. (maybe we can use [plato](https://github.com/es-analysis/plato) to measure this) - To gain valuable insights into the mental effort required by a developer to deliver the workload, you can measure task complexity by tracking number of dependencies, scope of work, level of technical challenge etc. You can also look at time spent by developers on context switching while multitasking, defect rate, time spent debugging, bug reopen rate, and time spent dealing with meetings, emails, and ad hoc requests. ### 3. - Satisfaction with time it takes to validate a local change

- Code review turnaround time

- Manual steps required to deploy a change
- frequency of documentation improvements | - Number of blocks of time without meeting or interruptions
- Frequency of unplanned tasks or requests
- Frequency of incidents requiring team attention | | KPIs (North star metrics) | - Overall perceived ease of delivering software
- Employee engagement or satisfaction
- Perceived productivity | | | ### Learn more about - KPIs (north start metrics) - [Space framework](https://www.hatica.io/blog/space-developer-productivity-metrics/) - How to measure code complexity - [pull requests](https://www.hatica.io/blog/pull-request-best-practices/) - [Code churn](https://www.hatica.io/blog/code-churn-rate/) ### Personal note - In my experience (and maybe its just me), but there is quite high number of times where I have a task returned from testing - This increases the cognitive load and context switching - Gives more work for me and testers - I need to think about how to minimize this (first thing that popped into my mind...better description of tasks) - I really think that component system (shadcn) that is used with combination of the designers and is properly tested is crucial to speed up development on the FE - Measuring perceived statistics to ensure dev ex satisfaction is important and "easy" to implement - ## Status

Draft

## Purpose

This document explains the direction behind the current frontend architecture work and ties three proposed RFCs into one technical vision:

- [[RFC-01 - Directed, Bounded Frontend Architecture]]
- [[RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing]]
- [[RFC-03 - Frontend Error Handeling]]

The goal is not to produce a perfect end-state up front. The goal is to create a codebase we can change with confidence while we are still learning what the product should become.

At this stage, these RFCs are primarily tools for team alignment and management sponsorship. They establish the direction and the operating model first. Some implementation details and success measures are still intentionally open and will be refined as the work moves from alignment into execution.

## Vision

We are building a frontend where each product capability is a locally understandable, independently testable, failure-contained slice that can be changed and released in small increments without requiring system-wide knowledge.

The outcome we want is a codebase engineers can trust. A developer should be able to open a feature, understand its boundaries, make a small change, verify the intended behaviour, and release it without fear that hidden coupling or unclear failure modes will break unrelated parts of the system.

We assume we do not know the final destination in advance. Frontend development is a learning process, so our operating loop is: experiment, get feedback, gain experience, then improve the design.

We will achieve that trust by simplifying the codebase, protecting clear boundaries, making feedback fast, containing failures close to where they happen, and shipping in smaller increments. We are optimizing for long-term development speed, not short-term local convenience.

## Why This Matters

The current codebase has grown organically. The main problems are not lack of effort or lack of talent. The problems are structural:

- unclear boundaries between responsibilities
- libraries that mix page composition, business logic, API calls, and shared UI
- sparse and inconsistent tests
- error handling that is not yet predictable enough for frequent releases
- too much hidden knowledge required to change code safely

These problems slow the team down in two ways at the same time:

1. They reduce trust, so developers become cautious and changes become larger and slower.
2. They increase accidental complexity, so even simple changes require more discovery, coordination, and rework than they should.

This vision is a response to both.

## Strategic Outcomes

### 1. Trust Through Local Reasoning

We want a codebase where most changes can be understood and verified locally.

A developer working on a feature should be able to answer:

- Where does this code belong?
- What is this slice allowed to import?
- What is the public API?
- How is this behaviour tested?
- Where will errors be handled if this fails?

If those answers are clear, people can work with confidence. If they are unclear, every change becomes a negotiation with hidden system behaviour.

### 2. Radical Simplicity

Simplicity does not mean fewer files or fewer abstractions by default. It means less accidental complexity.

We will prefer:

- explicit boundaries over convenience imports
- deep modules with narrow public APIs over shallow modules with many exports
- composition over cross-feature coupling
- clear documentation over tribal knowledge
- code that can be refactored daily over clever code that is hard to touch

The codebase should become easier to navigate, easier to explain, and easier to change.

### 3. Long-Term Development Speed

We are not trying to move slower in exchange for quality. We are treating quality as the mechanism that allows speed to persist.

Long-term speed comes from:

- small, safe refactorings
- acceptance tests that protect behaviour
- predictable error handling
- fewer hidden dependencies
- smaller blast radius when changes fail

This is how we avoid the common trap where local shortcuts create global drag.

### 4. Faster, More Incremental Releases

Frequent release is the integrating outcome of trust, simplicity, and feedback.

If features are isolated, behaviour is tested at the right boundary, and failures are recoverable, we can release smaller changes more often. Smaller releases produce faster feedback. Faster feedback improves learning. Better learning improves the product and the architecture at the same time.

## Architectural Direction

### Structural Direction: RFC-01

The library structure RFC gives us the structural precondition for trust.

Its main contribution is not folder naming. Its main contribution is making the dependency model explicit:

- code is organized by business responsibility, not technical type
- each slice has a narrow public API
- same-layer slices do not depend on each other
- shared code is truly shared and domain-free
- page code stays thin and compositional

This reduces hidden coupling and makes the codebase more discoverable. It also supports collective ownership because a developer can learn the system through consistent rules rather than through memory.

### Feedback Direction: RFC-02

The testing RFC gives us the feedback system that makes refactoring and incremental delivery safe.

Its main contribution is the testing boundary:

- test behaviour, not implementation
- test features through their public API
- mock only at the system boundary
- use Storybook as living documentation for visual layers
- treat acceptance criteria as the true coverage target

This gives us faster and more useful feedback than a large volume of brittle implementation tests. The purpose of tests is not to maximize numbers. The purpose is to make behavioural change visible quickly.

### Failure Containment Direction: RFC-03

The error handling RFC gives us predictable failure boundaries.

Its main contribution is controlling blast radius:

- expected errors are handled where the feature has enough context to respond
- unexpected errors are contained by error boundaries
- query and mutation errors follow explicit rules
- observability captures failures without crashing the application

This is essential for trust. Developers do not trust a system when one broken feature can take down an entire page, or when failures are hard to reason about. Containment and observability make experimentation safer.

## How The RFCs Work Together

These RFCs should be read as one operating model:

- RFC-01 creates clear boundaries.
- RFC-02 turns those boundaries into fast feedback loops.
- RFC-03 makes failures survivable and observable inside those boundaries.

Together they create the conditions for incremental design:

- small slices
- small interfaces
- small refactorings
- small releases
- small blast radius

The common theme is not structure for its own sake. The theme is making change cheaper and safer.

## Our Values, Principles, and Practices

### Values

#### Trust

Trust means developers can change the system with confidence.

A developer should be able to understand what a slice owns, what it exposes, how it is verified, and how it fails. Trust comes from explicit boundaries, useful feedback, and recoverable failures rather than from private knowledge or personal caution.

#### Simplicity

Simplicity means making the system easier to understand, navigate, and evolve.

We value explicit boundaries, small interfaces, clear documentation, and structures that support daily refactoring. We prefer straightforward designs that reduce accidental complexity over clever designs that save effort locally but increase confusion globally.

#### Learning

We accept that we do not know the final destination in advance.

Software development is a learning process. Our loop is: experiment, get feedback, gain experience, then improve the design. The codebase and the team practices should support this loop instead of pretending all important decisions can be known up front.

#### Sustainable Speed

We optimize for long-term development speed.

Speed is not the absence of discipline. Speed comes from a system that is easy to change, easy to verify, and easy to recover when something goes wrong. Quality is not the tradeoff against speed here; it is the mechanism that allows speed to persist.

#### Incremental Delivery

We prefer small releases, small refactorings, and reversible migration steps.

Smaller changes reduce risk, make feedback arrive sooner, and keep the team moving even while the architecture is evolving. We want architecture and delivery to reinforce each other, not compete with each other.

### Principles

#### Prefer Local Reasoning

Most changes should be understandable within the slice being changed. If a simple edit requires deep knowledge of unrelated parts of the system, the design is too coupled.

#### Make Boundaries Explicit

Public APIs, layer rules, ownership, and failure boundaries are part of the design. They should be visible in the structure, enforced in tooling, and understandable from the code itself.

#### Keep Interfaces Small and Modules Deep

We want slices that hide internal complexity behind narrow interfaces. A large implementation behind a small public API is easier to evolve than many small abstractions that leak internal details.

#### Treat Behaviour As The Contract

The real contract of a feature is what users can observe and rely on. Tests, stories, and documentation should reflect behaviour first, not current implementation details.

#### Use Feedback To Build Confidence

Confidence should come from acceptance tests, visual checks, CI, observability, and production feedback. We do not trust a change because it feels correct. We trust it because the system tells us enough to believe it.

#### Contain Failure

Failures should be handled at the smallest level that can respond meaningfully. A broken feature should not take down an entire page. An unexpected error should be observable and recoverable, not mysterious.

#### Surface Problems Early

Problems in the system should become visible quickly.

This includes failing code, weak boundaries, confusing structure, slow feedback loops, process friction, and recurring patterns of incorrect implementation. If an issue stays hidden, it stays expensive. We want a culture and a codebase where raising problems is normal, encouraged, and expected.

The goal is not to create blame. The goal is to shorten the time between a problem appearing and the team learning about it clearly enough to improve the system.

#### Improve Continuously

Refactoring is daily work. We do not postpone clarity until a future cleanup phase. When we touch code, we should leave its structure, naming, and boundaries better than we found them.

#### Prefer Reversible Steps Over Big-Bang Moves

The codebase should evolve through small, safe, composable steps. We would rather learn through a sequence of small migrations than through one large rewrite that delays feedback.

#### Document Intent Close To The Code

Readmes, stories, explicit exports, and clear folder structure reduce discovery cost. Understanding how to use a slice should not depend on private team memory.

### Practices

These practices are how we make the values and principles real.

#### Organize By Business Responsibility

We organize code by layers and slices, not by generic technical folders. Shared code stays domain-free, pages stay thin, and business capabilities live in features with clear ownership.

#### Enforce Public APIs And Dependency Rules

Each slice exposes a narrow public API. Internal files stay internal. Import direction is enforced so that boundaries remain real under delivery pressure.

#### Test At The Right Boundary

We test behaviour and outcomes, not implementation details.

Feature tests should verify user-observable results through the feature's public API. Pure logic gets direct unit tests when it is subtle enough to deserve them. Visual states are captured through stories.

We should test often and run tests often while developing, not only at the end of the task. Test-first approaches are advised because they sharpen the expected behaviour early, but they are not enforced as ceremony.

Every task that changes the behaviour of the system, feature, or UI should add or update tests that describe the intended result. The important question is not "did we test this function internally?" but "did we verify the behaviour that users, developers, or other parts of the system rely on?"

#### Treat Errors As Part Of The Design

Expected errors are designed into the feature experience. Unexpected errors are contained by boundaries, reported through observability, and handled so users can recover wherever possible.

#### Refactor In Small Amounts Every Day

We make incremental design part of normal work. Simplifying names, shrinking interfaces, clarifying ownership, and removing coupling are regular tasks, not exceptional events.

#### Release In Small Steps

We prefer smaller PRs and smaller releases that can be understood, reviewed, tested, and rolled back more easily. Release frequency is a product of trust, not a separate initiative.

#### Keep Documentation Close And Practical

Each slice should explain what it is, what it exports, and how it is intended to be used. Documentation should reduce the cost of change, not exist as a parallel system that drifts from the code.

#### Keep CI Close To The Change

Typechecking, linting, tests, and story-level verification should run as part of the normal PR path so that feedback arrives while the change is still small.

#### Improve The System While Delivering Features

Migration and cleanup are not separate from product work. When a slice is opened for change, that is an opportunity to simplify it, document it, test it, and align it with the direction of the system.

## Decision Filters

When making architectural or implementation decisions, we should ask:

1. Does this make the code easier to understand locally?
2. Does this reduce or increase the public interface surface?
3. Does this create faster feedback for future changes?
4. Does this reduce blast radius when something fails?
5. Does this help us ship smaller changes more frequently?

If the answer is no to most of these questions, the change is probably moving us away from the vision.

## Near-Term Focus

In the near term, success means:

1. establish the FSD boundaries and import rules from RFC-01
2. build the testing infrastructure and conventions from RFC-02
3. implement the shared error handling infrastructure from RFC-03
4. migrate incrementally, using each migrated slice as an opportunity to simplify, document, and test

The immediate goal is not architectural purity. The immediate goal is to create a stable pattern that increases confidence with each migration step.

To turn this direction into an executable plan, we still need to define a more measurable end state, make more specific technology choices around the testing stack, and align with UX on recovery expectations for user-facing error states.

## Summary

This vision is simple:

We want a frontend codebase that is easy to understand, safe to change, and fast to release.

The way to get there is also simple:

- explicit boundaries
- small interfaces
- fast feedback
- recoverable failures
- continuous refactoring
- incremental releases

This is not only an architectural direction. It is a team operating model rooted in trust, simplicity, learning, sustainable speed, and incremental delivery.

id: Feedback loops make change survivable
aliases: []

## Claim

Change is inevitable in software, so the real question is how quickly a team can detect reality and adapt.

## Notes

- Requirements, design, technology, and team context all keep moving.
- Long feedback cycles make every decision more speculative and every correction more expensive.
- XP treats practices as experiments that should improve effectiveness, communication, confidence, and productivity.
- Feedback can come from tests, code, deployment, teammates, customers, and production behaviour.
- The point is not maximal process, but the shortest feedback loop the team can use well.

## Implications

- Prefer minutes or hours over weeks or months when validating changes.
- A healthy engineering system makes problems visible early.
- Adaptation is a first-class skill, not a sign of poor planning.

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/Extreme Programming Explained Embrace Change#^xp-feedback]]

- [[Technical Vision]]

id: Plans are coordination tools, not predictions
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Extreme Programming Explained]]"
---

## Claim

Plans are valuable because they help teams coordinate action with current knowledge, not because they can reliably predict a changing future.

## Notes

- Cost and value estimates are uncertain and change as work progresses.
- Planning works best when it happens at multiple cadences: daily, weekly, and longer-term.
- The plan should change as facts change.
- Making decisions later can improve quality because more information is available.
- Short cycles limit the scope of slips and keep the highest-value work moving first.

## Implications

- Treat planning as a living process rather than a commitment to yesterday's assumptions.
- Use plans to align priorities and tradeoffs, not to deny uncertainty.
- Keep release slices small enough that missed estimates do not become existential.

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/Extreme Programming Explained Embrace Change#^xp-plans-not-predictions]]

## Related

- [[FE Design System Scaling Web Applications]]

id: Quality is a speed strategy, not a tradeoff
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Extreme Programming Explained]]"
---

## Claim

Reducing quality to move faster is usually self-defeating; higher quality often makes delivery faster and more predictable.

## Notes

- Quality is not a control variable you can tune down without consequences.
- Defects, weak design, and brittle delivery systems create drag that compounds over time.
- Teams move faster when they can trust the code, the tests, and the release path.
- Pride in the work matters too: quality is also a human need, not just an economic one.

## Implications

- Treat design quality, test quality, and release quality as enablers of speed.
- Avoid false tradeoffs where "faster now" really means "slower later with less certainty."
- Raise quality through small, continuous improvements instead of waiting for perfect conditions.

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/Extreme Programming Explained Embrace Change#^xp-quality-speed]]

## Related

- [[Strategic programming treats design work as an investment]]
- [[RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing]]

id: Software complexity comes from dependencies and obscurity
aliases: []
indexes:
  - '[[3 - Indexes/A Philosophy of Software Design]]'
---

## Claim

Software becomes hard to change for two main reasons: behavior depends on too many connected parts, or the important information is not obvious.

## Notes

- **Dependencies** create change amplification: one small change fans out across many files or modules.
- **Obscurity** creates unknown unknowns: it is unclear what matters, what is safe to touch, or what hidden assumption exists.
- Complexity is not just about size; a larger design can be simpler if it lowers [[developer velocity|cognitive load]] and makes change local.
- Complexity should be judged from the reader's perspective, because maintainability is a team property.

## Signals

- A small feature requires edits in multiple places.
- You need to keep too much context in your head.
- It is hard to know whether a change is actually complete.
- Bugs appear because a hidden rule was encoded somewhere unexpected.

## Implications

- Favor designs that keep knowledge local.
- Prefer obviousness over compactness.
- Measure architecture by the cost of modification, not by elegance alone.

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/A Philosophy of Software Design#^aposd-dependencies-obscurity]]

### For believe me! - the secret for harvesting from existence the greatest fruitfulness and the greatest enjoyment is: To live dangerously!

- Illusion of self improvement. Where constant self improvements works as a escape from not facing your sickness. Follow your heart, trust you [[impulse]]s, be spontaneous.
- In order to be healthy we must first experience sickness.
- How can you rise anew if you have not first become ashes?
- Is great health inherently a paradox? As the worst kind of sickness is stagnation.
- "I love him who has a free spirit and a free heart: thus his head only the guts of his hear; his hear, however, causes his down-going"
- "I wish to see as beautiful what is necessary in things. Thus I will be one of those who make things beautiful. Amor fati: let that be my love henceforth. I do not wish to wage war against the ugly. I do not wish to accuse; I don not even wish to accuse those who accuse. Looking away shall be my only negation. And, all in all and on the whole: some day I wish to be only a Yes-sayer!"

import re
for p in sorted(Path('2 - Main Notes').glob('*.md')):
    text = p.read_text(errors='ignore')
    m = re.search(r'^## Claim\n\n(.+?)(?:\n\n|$)', text, re.M|re.S)
    claim = m.group(1).strip().replace('\n',' ') if m else ''
    print(f'{p.name}\t{claim}')
PY

content.text:
API choices should start from consumers and communication boundaries.md	The best API style depends first on who is communicating and what boundary the API must serve.
Async await can be modeled as generator execution resumed by promise resolution.md	One way to understand `async`/`await` is as paused execution that resumes when a promise resolves, similar to how a generator can yield and later continue with a value.
Async callbacks resume through different task queues depending on their source.md	Asynchronous callbacks do not all resume through the same queue: promise reactions run as microtasks, while timers, many I/O callbacks, and scheduled browser tasks run as macrotasks.
Binary Search.md	
Box sizing can be intrinsic or constrained.md	A box can size itself from its own content or have its size constrained by CSS rules and surrounding layout context.
Box type determines formatting context and sizing behavior.md	Whether a box behaves as block-level, inline-level, or anonymous changes how it flows, how it sizes, and which formatting rules apply.
CSS class specificity.md	
Closure.md	A closure is a function that keeps access to variables from the lexical scope where it was created, even after that outer scope has finished executing.
Cognitive load is a developer experience problem not just a code complexity problem.md	Cognitive load in developer experience comes from both code complexity and the surrounding working environment.
Comments should capture non-obvious design knowledge.md	Comments are most useful when they preserve intent, constraints, and assumptions that the code itself cannot fully express.
Complete Pipeline.md	
Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS).md	
DOM mutations are expensive because they can trigger layout and paint work.md	DOM changes are expensive because they can trigger multiple browser pipeline steps, especially when they affect layout.
DOM querying performance depends on API choice selector scope and collection behavior.md	DOM query performance depends not just on selector complexity but also on whether the API returns cached live structures, copied collections, or ID-based lookups.
Data models should fit relationship shape and access patterns.md	The right data model depends on how connected the data is and how it is read together, not on universal preference for documents, tables, or graphs.
Deep modules hide complexity behind simple interfaces.md	A module is valuable when it hides substantial implementation complexity behind an interface that is easy to learn and hard to misuse.
Developer experience can be understood through feedback loops cognitive load and flow state.md	A useful model of developer experience has three core dimensions: feedback loops, cognitive load, and flow state.
Event Loop.md	The event loop only moves queued work onto the call stack after synchronous execution is finished, and it prioritizes microtasks before macrotasks.
FE Design System Real Time Updates.md	
FE System Design - RADIO.md	
Fast feedback loops are a core dimension of developer experience.md	Fast feedback loops improve developer experience because they shorten the time between making a change and learning whether it worked.
Feedback loops make change survivable.md	Change is inevitable in software, so the real question is how quickly a team can detect reality and adapt.
First Contentful Paint (FCP).md	
Flow state depends on protecting uninterrupted focus and reducing reactive work.md	Developer flow state depends on protecting long enough blocks of focused work while reducing interruptions, unplanned tasks, and reactive demands.
Formatting contexts make layout rules local and predictable.md	Formatting contexts make browser layout predictable by giving groups of elements their own local layout rules.
Frontend scaling has architectural and codebase dimensions.md	Scaling frontend work is partly an architecture problem and partly a code-organization problem inside each application.
Generators let JavaScript pause and resume execution.md	Generators are functions whose execution can pause at `yield` and later resume from the same point.
GraphQL favors client-shaped queries for complex data relationships.md	GraphQL is strongest when clients need flexible, schema-driven access to complex and connected data shapes.
Hoisting is an imperfect mental model for declaration timing and TDZ.md	Hoisting is a useful shortcut for discussing declaration timing, but it is an imperfect explanation for how scope setup and initialization actually work.
Incremental design keeps the cost of change low.md	The goal is not to avoid design until later, but to keep redesigning in small steps as experience reveals what the system really needs.
Interaction to Next Paint (INP).md	
Iterators.md	Iterators turn stateful traversal into a standardized step-by-step protocol.
JavaScript coercion follows abstract conversion operations.md	JavaScript coercion is not random; it follows abstract conversion rules such as `ToPrimitive`, `ToNumber`, `ToString`, and `ToBoolean`.
JavaScript equality depends on coercion rules not loose-versus-strict slogans.md	The difference between `==` and `===` is best understood through coercion rules, not the oversimplified slogan of "loose versus strict" equality.
JavaScript values include primitives objects and special edge cases.md	JavaScript values are easiest to reason about when primitives, objects, and edge-case values are treated as distinct categories with different behaviors.
Largest Contentful Paint (LCP).md	
Layer compositing improves some rendering work but increases memory cost.md	Composited layers can make some visual changes smoother and cheaper, but creating extra layers consumes memory and should be done deliberately.
Legacy load metrics are useful but incomplete.md	Legacy browser load metrics such as `DOMContentLoaded` and `load` are still useful, but they do not fully describe the quality of user experience.
Lexical scope determines where JavaScript looks for identifiers.md	JavaScript is lexically scoped, which means identifier visibility is determined by where code is written, not where functions are later called.
Maintainability means being able to change code confidently.md	A maintainable codebase is one where engineers can change behavior, refactor structure, and update dependencies without overwhelming fear.
Micro-frontends trade independent delivery for operational complexity.md	Micro-frontends can improve team autonomy and release independence, but they introduce new complexity in coordination, performance, tooling, and user experience consistency.
Mocking code you own often signals poor separation of concerns.md	If tests regularly need to mock your own application code, that is often evidence that responsibilities are not separated cleanly enough.
Modular monoliths preserve boundaries without distributed deployment.md	A modular monolith captures many of the boundary benefits teams want from distributed architectures while keeping deployment and tooling simpler.
Mono Repo.md	
Monoliths optimize for simplicity before scale.md	A monolithic frontend is often the simplest starting point, but its simplicity erodes as team size, system complexity, and change volume increase.
No file.md	A closure preserves access to variables from lexical scope by reference, not by copying their current values.
Nx.md	
Percentiles reveal performance risk better than averages.md	Average performance hides important user experience problems, while percentiles show how bad the experience is for significant groups of users.
Performance goals depend on user context and the value being delivered.md	How fast is fast enough depends on who the users are, what they are trying to do, and how much value they expect from the page or action.
Performance instrumentation should combine browser APIs vitals and RUM.md	Good performance measurement combines browser-level instrumentation, standardized user-centric metrics, and real-user monitoring in production.
Plans are coordination tools, not predictions.md	Plans are valuable because they help teams coordinate action with current knowledge, not because they can reliably predict a changing future.
Positioning and stacking contexts change how elements participate in layout.md	Positioning changes whether elements follow normal flow, and stacking contexts introduce a layering model beyond the page's default x-y layout.
Promises let JavaScript resume work after external operations finish.md	Promises let JavaScript represent future values now and attach continuation logic that runs later when external work completes.
Prototypes implement delegation rather than classical inheritance.md	JavaScript object behavior is best understood as prototype delegation rather than classical inheritance.
Quality is a speed strategy, not a tradeoff.md	Reducing quality to move faster is usually self-defeating; higher quality often makes delivery faster and more predictable.
REST favors standard resource-oriented HTTP APIs.md	REST is strongest when APIs can be modeled as standard HTTP resources with predictable caching and loose coupling.
RPC favors action-oriented and strongly typed service contracts.md	RPC is useful when the API is better described as callable actions than as resource state.
React Patterns.md	
Reliable systems tolerate faults instead of pretending faults disappear.md	Reliability comes less from eliminating all faults and more from designing systems so local faults do not become user-visible failures.
Rendering pipeline flows through render tree layout paint and compositing.md	Browsers render pages by combining structure and styles into a render tree, computing layout, painting pixels, and then compositing layers together.
Replication and partitioning solve different scaling problems.md	Replication and partitioning are both distribution techniques, but one duplicates data for availability and read scale while the other splits data to spread storage and load.
Scalability depends on explicit load and performance assumptions.md	A system cannot be said to scale in the abstract; it scales only relative to specific load patterns and performance goals.
Small batches preserve flow and reduce integration risk.md	Software delivery becomes more stable when integration, testing, and deployment happen in small batches rather than in large delayed merges.
Software complexity comes from dependencies and obscurity.md	Software becomes hard to change for two main reasons: behavior depends on too many connected parts, or the important information is not obvious.
Storage engines are workload tradeoffs, not universal winners.md	Storage and indexing designs should be judged by workload tradeoffs, because write-optimized, read-optimized, transactional, and analytical systems make different promises.
Strategic programming treats design work as an investment.md	The right unit of optimization is not today's delivery speed but the long-term cost of future changes.
Test types trade speed for realism and confidence.md	Different test types are valuable because they trade execution speed against realism and confidence in different ways.
Test-first development is a design tool.md	Writing the test first is useful not only because it verifies behaviour, but because it pressures the design toward clearer interfaces and tighter scope.
The Cult of DONE.md	
Time to Interactive (TTI).md	
Total Blocking Time (TBT).md	
Transactions simplify applications by moving guarantees into the database.md	Transactions are valuable because they move certain coordination and correctness guarantees out of application code and into the database.
Web performance should be judged by user perception and business outcomes.md	Performance matters because it changes what users feel, whether they stay, and whether business goals are achieved.
Web performance should be measured with field and lab data for different questions.md	Lab data and field data are both necessary because they answer different performance questions.
Well-architected UI applications need patterns processes and systems.md	Frontend quality comes from a layered system: patterns shape the code, processes shape decisions, and systems enforce feedback.
gRPC is strongest for high-performance service-to-service communication.md	gRPC is most compelling when services need fast, typed, efficient communication under high load.
tRPC is strongest when one TypeScript team owns both client and server.md	tRPC is strongest when a private API is owned end-to-end by teams working in one TypeScript system.
this is determined by call-site except in arrow functions.md	In ordinary JavaScript functions, `this` is determined by how a function is called, while arrow functions inherit `this` lexically.

./4 - Source Material/Books/The Ethics.md:16:- PROP. XXIX. Nothing in the universe is contingent, but all things are conditioned to exist and operate in a particular manner by the necessity of the divine nature. Proof.—Whatsoever is, is in God (Prop. xv.). But God cannot be called a thing contingent. For (by Prop. xi.) he exists necessarily, and not contingently. Further, the modes of the divine nature follow therefrom necessarily, and not contingently (Prop. xvi.); and they thus follow, whether we consider the divine nature absolutely, or whether we consider it as in any way conditioned to act (Prop. xxvii.). Further, God is not only the cause of these modes, in so far as they simply exist (by Prop. xxiv, Coroll.), but also in so far as they are considered as conditioned for operating in a particular manner (Prop. xxvi.). If they be not conditioned by God (Prop. xxvi.), it is impossible, and not contingent, that they should condition themselves; contrariwise, if they be conditioned by God, it is impossible, and not contingent, that they should render themselves unconditioned. Wherefore all things are conditioned by the necessity of the divine nature, not only to exist, but also to exist and operate in a particular manner, and there is nothing that is contingent. Q.E.D. Note.—Before going any further, I wish here to explain, what we should understand by nature viewed as active (natura naturans), and nature viewed as passive (natura naturata). I say to explain, or rather call attention to it, for I think that, from what has been said, it is sufficiently clear, that by nature viewed as active we should understand that which is in itself, and is conceived through itself, or those attributes of substance, which express eternal and infinite essence, in other words (Prop. xiv., Coroll. i., and Prop. xvii., Coroll. ii) God, in so far as he is considered as a free cause. By nature viewed as passive I understand all that which follows from the necessity of the nature of God, or of any of the attributes of God, that is, all the modes of the attributes of God, in so far as they are considered as things which are in God, and which without God cannot exist or be conceived. ([Location 394](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B01N4VM71P&location=394))
./4 - Source Material/Books/The Ethics.md:17:- PROP. XXXI. The intellect in function, whether finite or infinite, as will, desire, love, &c., should be referred to passive nature and not to active nature. Proof.—By the intellect we do not (obviously) mean absolute thought, but only a certain mode of thinking, differing from other modes, such as love, desire, &c., and therefore (Def. v.) requiring to be conceived through absolute thought. It must (by Prop. xv. and Def. vi.), through some attribute of God which expresses the eternal and infinite essence of thought, be so conceived, that without such attribute it could neither be nor be conceived. It must therefore be referred to nature passive rather than to nature active, as must also the other modes of thinking. Q.E.D. Note.—I do not here, by speaking of intellect in function, admit that there is such a thing as intellect in potentiality: but, wishing to avoid all confusion, I desire to speak only of what is most clearly perceived by us, namely, of the very act of understanding, than which nothing is more clearly perceived. For we cannot perceive anything without adding to our knowledge of the act of understanding. PROP. XXXII. Will cannot be called a free cause, but only a necessary cause. Proof.—Will is only a particular mode of thinking, like intellect; therefore (by Prop. xxviii.) no volition can exist, nor be conditioned to act, unless it be conditioned by some cause other than itself, which cause is conditioned by a third cause, and so on to infinity. But if will be supposed infinite, it must also be conditioned to exist and act by God, not by virtue of his being substance absolutely infinite, but by virtue of his possessing an attribute which expresses the infinite and eternal essence of thought (by Prop. xxiii.). Thus, however it be conceived, whether as finite or infinite, it requires a cause by which it should be conditioned to exist and act. Thus (Def. vii.) it cannot be called a free cause, but only a necessary or constrained cause. Q.E.D. Coroll. I.—Hence it follows, first, that God does not act according to freedom of the will. Coroll. II.—It follows, secondly, that will and intellect stand in the same relation to the nature of God as do motion, and rest, and absolutely all natural phenomena, which must be conditioned by God (Prop. xxix.) to exist and act in a particular manner. For will, like the rest, stands in need of a cause, by which it is conditioned to exist and act in a particular manner. And although, when will or intellect be granted, an infinite number of results may follow, yet God cannot on that account be said to act from freedom of the will, any more than the infinite number of results from motion and rest would justify us in saying that motion and rest act by free will. Wherefore will no more appertains to God than does anything else in nature, but stands in the same relation to him as motion, rest, and the like, which we have shown to follow from the necessity of the divine nature, and to be conditioned by it… ([Location 415](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B01N4VM71P&location=415))
./4 - Source Material/Books/The Ethics.md:37:- PROP. I. Our mind is in certain cases active, and in certain cases passive. In so far as it has adequate ideas it is necessarily active, and in so far as it has inadequate ideas, it is necessarily passive. Proof.—In every human mind there are some adequate ideas, and some ideas that are fragmentary and confused (II. xl. note). Those ideas which are adequate in the mind are adequate also in God, inasmuch as he constitutes the essence of the mind (II. xl. Coroll.), and those which are inadequate in the mind are likewise (by the same Coroll.) adequate in God, not inasmuch as he contains in himself the essence of the given mind alone, but as he, at the same time, contains the minds of other things. Again, from any given idea some effect must necessarily follow (I. 36); of this effect God is the adequate cause (III. Def. i.), not inasmuch as he is infinite, but inasmuch as he is conceived as affected by the given idea (II. ix.). But of that effect whereof God is the cause, inasmuch as he is affected by an idea which is adequate in a given mind, of that effect, I repeat, the mind in question is the adequate cause (II. xi. Coroll.). Therefore our mind, in so far as it has adequate ideas (III. Def. ii.), is in certain cases necessarily active; this was our first point. Again, whatsoever necessarily follows from the idea which is adequate in God, not by virtue of his possessing in himself the mind of one man only, but by virtue of his containing, together with the mind of that one man, the minds of other things also, of such an effect (II. xi. Coroll.) the mind of the given man is not an adequate, but only a partial cause; thus (III. Def. ii.) the mind, inasmuch as it has inadequate ideas, is in certain cases necessarily passive; this was our second point. Therefore our mind, &c. Q.E.D. Corollary.—Hence it follows that the mind is more or less liable to be acted upon, in proportion as it possesses inadequate ideas, and, contrariwise, is more or less active in proportion as it possesses adequate ideas. ([Location 1417](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B01N4VM71P&location=1417))
./4 - Source Material/Books/The Ethics.md:79:- PROP. XV. Desire arising from the knowledge of good and bad can be quenched or checked by many of the other desires arising from the emotions whereby we are assailed. Proof.—From the true knowledge of good and evil, in so far as it is an emotion, necessarily arises desire (Def. of the Emotions, i.), the strength of which is proportioned to the strength of the emotion wherefrom it arises (III. xxxvii.). But, inasmuch as this desire arises (by hypothesis) from the fact of our truly understanding anything, it follows that it is also present with us, in so far as we are active (III. i.), and must therefore be understood through our essence only (III. Def. ii.); consequently (III. vii.) its force and increase can be defined solely by human power. Again, the desires arising from the emotions whereby we are assailed are stronger, in proportion as the said emotions are more vehement; wherefore their force and increase must be defined solely by the power of external causes, which, when compared with our own power, indefinitely surpass it (IV. iii.); hence the desires arising from like emotions may be more vehement, than the desire which arises from a true knowledge of good and evil, and may, consequently, control or quench it. Q.E.D. ([Location 2556](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B01N4VM71P&location=2556))
./4 - Source Material/Books/The Ethics.md:80:- PROP. XXIII. Man, in so far as he is determined to a particular action because he has inadequate ideas, cannot be absolutely said to act in obedience to virtue; he can only be so described, in so far as he is determined for the action because he understands. Proof.—In so far as a man is determined to an action through having inadequate ideas, he is passive (III. i.), that is (III. Deff. i., and iii.), he does something, which cannot be perceived solely through his essence, that is (by IV. Def. viii.), which does not follow from his virtue. But, in so far as he is determined for an action because he understands, he is active; that is, he does something, which is perceived through his essence alone, or which adequately follows from his virtue. Q.E.D. ([Location 2641](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B01N4VM71P&location=2641))
./4 - Source Material/Books/The Ethics.md:88:- no emotion, whereof we cannot form some clear and distinct conception. For an emotion is the idea of a modification of the body (by the general Def. of the Emotions), and must therefore (by the preceding Prop.) involve some clear and distinct conception. Note.—Seeing that there is nothing which is not followed by an effect (I. xxxvi.), and that we clearly and distinctly understand whatever follows from an idea, which in us is adequate (II. xl.), it follows that everyone has the power of clearly and distinctly understanding himself and his emotions, if not absolutely, at any rate in part, and consequently of bringing it about, that he should become less subject to them. To attain this result, therefore, we must chiefly direct our efforts to acquiring, as far as possible, a clear and distinct knowledge of every emotion, in order that the mind may thus, through emotion, be determined to think of those things which it clearly and distinctly perceives, and wherein it fully acquiesces: and thus that the emotion itself may be separated from the thought of an external cause, and may be associated with true thoughts; whence it will come to pass, not only that love, hatred, &c. will be destroyed (V. ii.), but also that the appetites or desires, which are wont to arise from such emotion, will become incapable of being excessive (IV. lxi.). For it must be especially remarked, that the appetite through which a man is said to be active, and that through which he is said to be passive is one and the same. For instance, we have shown that human nature is so constituted, that everyone desires his fellow—men to live after his own fashion (III. xxxi. note); in a man, who is not guided by reason, this appetite is a passion which is called ambition, and does not greatly differ from pride; whereas in a man, who lives by the dictates of reason, it is an activity or virtue which is called piety (IV. xxxvii. note. i. and second proof). In like manner all appetites or desires are only passions, in so far as they spring from inadequate ideas; the same results are accredited to virtue, when they are aroused or generated by adequate ideas. For all desires, whereby we are determined to any given action, may arise as much from adequate as from inadequate ideas (IV. lix.). Than this remedy for the emotions (to return to the point from which I started), which consists in a true knowledge thereof, nothing more excellent, being within our power, can be devised. For the mind has no other power save that of thinking and of forming adequate ideas, as we have shown above (III. iii.). ([Location 3382](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B01N4VM71P&location=3382))
./4 - Source Material/Books/The Ethics.md:91:- I. In the actual knowledge of the emotions (V. iv. note). II. In the fact that it separates the emotions from the thought of an external cause, which we conceive confusedly (V. ii. and V. iv. note). III. In the fact, that, in respect to time, the emotions referred to things, which we distinctly understand, surpass those referred to what we conceive in a confused and fragmentary manner (V. vii.). IV. In the number of causes whereby those modifications17 are fostered, which have regard to the common properties of things or to God (V. ix. xi.). V. Lastly, in the order wherein the mind can arrange and associate, one with another, its own emotions (V. x. note and xii. xiii. xiv.). But, in order that this power of the mind over the emotions may be better understood, it should be specially observed that the emotions are called by us strong, when we compare the emotion of one man with the emotion of another, and see that one man is more troubled than another by the same emotion; or when we are comparing the various emotions of the same man one with another, and find that he is more affected or stirred by one emotion than by another. For the strength of every emotion is defined by a comparison of our own power with the power of an external cause. Now the power of the mind is defined by knowledge only, and its infirmity or passion is defined by the privation of knowledge only: it therefore follows, that that mind is most passive, whose greatest part is made up of inadequate ideas, so that it may be characterized more readily by its passive states than by its activities: on the other hand, that mind is most active, whose greatest part is made up of adequate ideas, so that, although it may contain as many inadequate ideas as the former mind, it may yet be more easily characterized by ideas attributable to human virtue, than by ideas which tell of human infirmity. Again, it must be observed, that spiritual unhealthiness and misfortunes can generally be traced to excessive love for something which is subject to many variations, and which we can never become masters of. For no one is solicitous or anxious about anything, unless he loves it; neither do wrongs, suspicions, enmities, &c. arise, except in regard to things whereof no one can be really master. We may thus readily conceive the power which clear and distinct knowledge, and especially that third kind of knowledge (II. xlvii. note), founded on the actual knowledge of God, possesses over the emotions: if it does not absolutely destroy them, in so far as they are passions (V. iii. and iv. note); at any rate, it causes them to occupy a very small part of the mind (V. xiv.). Further, it begets a love towards a thing immutable and eternal (V. xv.), whereof we may really enter into possession (II. xlv.); neither can it be defiled with those faults which are inherent in ordinary love; but it may grow from strength to strength, and may engross the greater part of the mind, and deeply penetrate it. And now I… ([Location 3526](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B01N4VM71P&location=3526))
./4 - Source Material/Books/Spinoza.md:32:- This diminishment of the affects of love and hate, praise and blame, is the basis of one of the key ways in which Spinoza says we may free ourselves from the power of harmful affects. By coming to see that stupid Billy from Chapter 4 as determined in his bullying ways, the presupposition of freedom on which my negative affects of blame and hate were erected is undermined. To that extent, those negative affects slip away But why, one might ask, isn’t the negative affect now simply transferred from Billy to the whole deterministic series of causes, leading perhaps back to God, a series which I now see as causing the harmful effects? In this way, the affects of blame and hate would be shifted, but would still remain as strong as before. Spinoza responds to this difficulty by pointing out that my realization of the determination of Billy’s actions is my way of having a more adequate idea of those actions and thus of the passions they caused in me. To the extent that I now enjoy this broader view, I am more active and so happier. The negative affects of blame and hate give way, at least to some extent, to the active joys of understanding. (There is a similar line of argument in 5p 18 for the claim that no one can hate God.) Spinoza seems to think that the passive affects of blame and hate can be eliminated in this way (5p3). However, it’s not clear why this would be true, especially given that Billy has in fact harmed me, decreased my power of acting. Nevertheless, the harm may be compensated for to some degree by the benefits of understanding, and this may be enough for Spinoza to show that we can gain some measure of control over our passive affects. Whether or not this is an effective strategy for overcoming the affects, Spinoza can be seen as expressing a genuine and Freudian insight: passions may lose some of their power to harm us when we have a clearer understanding of how we came to have them and to be in their power. ([Location 3048](https://readwise.io/to_kindle?action=open&asin=B000SMD1E2&location=3048))

content.text:
---
id: Flow state depends on protecting uninterrupted focus and reducing reactive work
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Developer Experience]]"
---

## Claim

Developer flow state depends on protecting long enough blocks of focused work while reducing interruptions, unplanned tasks, and reactive demands.

## Notes

- Flow state is harmed by meetings, calls, incidents, ad hoc requests, and unclear goals.
- Useful measures include uninterrupted time blocks, frequency of unplanned work, and disruptive incident load.
- Perception matters too: developers need to feel able to focus and understand what success looks like.
- Flow state is not just personal discipline; it is shaped by team process and environment.

## Implications

- Teams should treat interruption management as a systems problem.
- Better task clarity and fewer surprise demands improve developer focus.
- Flow can be improved by protecting time, reducing meeting overhead, and limiting reactive churn.

## Related

- [[Developer experience can be understood through feedback loops cognitive load and flow state]]
- [[Technical Vision]]
- [[Quality is a speed strategy, not a tradeoff]]

content.thinking:
**Exploring philosophical connections**

I'm considering how to connect Nietzsche's concepts of reactive and active with Spinoza's active and passive ideas. I think I might search for terms like "adequate" to find more hidden philosophical connections. It could lead me to "understanding," "clear and distinct," and "passive," checking if these link to my tech notes. There's also an idea here about how Spinoza frames freedom as understanding, which aligns with my notes on maintainability. I may want to compare these insights further.

**Examining design principles**

I'm thinking of locality as a universal principle that seems to apply across various contexts like CSS formatting, modular monoliths, and transactions. Then there’s the idea of resumption and continuation, which relates to constructs like iterators, promises, and async-await, though it might be a bit too broad. Measurement skepticism stands out too—emphasizing percentiles instead of averages and valuing developer experience over mere output metrics. I also want to dig into surface and containment, looking for evidence in error boundaries and formatted layouts. Finally, I see potential in using philosophy to bridge back to flow state.

content.text:
---
id: Formatting contexts make layout rules local and predictable
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/FE Design System Web Rendering Patterns]]"
---

## Claim

Formatting contexts make browser layout predictable by giving groups of elements their own local layout rules.

## Notes

- Formatting contexts can be nested.
- Their main value is isolation: elements inside a context are governed by that context's rules instead of arbitrary outside interference.
- They also improve scalability and predictability because introducing a new layout rule set can be done by creating a different context such as flex or grid.
- Common contexts in the note source were block, inline, flex, and grid.
- Some elements create their own formatting contexts by default, while CSS can also override default behavior.

## Implications

- Layout bugs are easier to reason about when you first identify the active formatting context.
- Flex and grid are not just layout utilities; they establish different rule systems.
- Predictable layout comes from understanding local rules, not only from memorizing CSS properties.

## Related

- [[Box type determines formatting context and sizing behavior]]
- [[FE Design System Web Rendering Patterns]]
- [[Rendering pipeline flows through render tree layout paint and compositing]]

---
id: Deep modules hide complexity behind simple interfaces
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/A Philosophy of Software Design]]"
---

## Claim

A module is valuable when it hides substantial implementation complexity behind an interface that is easy to learn and hard to misuse.

## Notes

- A **deep module** offers a lot of capability through a small public surface.
- A **shallow module** adds ceremony without removing much complexity.
- Interface simplicity matters more than implementation simplicity because callers pay the interface cost repeatedly.
- Information hiding and deep modules reinforce each other: the more knowledge a module keeps internal, the more freedom the rest of the system has.

## Design test

Ask: does this abstraction reduce what the caller must know, or does it simply move code around?

## Signals of shallow design

- Pass-through methods
- Overexposed APIs
- Many exports with weak cohesion
- Consumers importing internal details to get real work done

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/A Philosophy of Software Design#^aposd-deep-modules]]

## Related

- [[RFC-01 - Directed, Bounded Frontend Architecture]]
- [[RFC-03 - Frontend Error Handeling]]
- [[FE Design System Scaling Web Applications]]

---
id: Transactions simplify applications by moving guarantees into the database
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Designing Data-Intensive Applications]]"
---

## Claim

Transactions are valuable because they move certain coordination and correctness guarantees out of application code and into the database.

## Notes

- Without transactional guarantees, application developers have to reason about subtle failure and replication edge cases themselves.
- Stronger guarantees simplify application logic because more invariants can be trusted.
- ACID is useful as vocabulary, but implementations vary and the term can hide important differences.
- Consistency in ACID is largely about application-defined invariants, not magic correctness provided by the database.

## Implications

- Evaluate databases by their actual guarantees, not by marketing labels alone.
- Put correctness responsibilities at the lowest sensible layer.
- Treat transactions as a tool for simplifying applications, not just as a database feature checkbox.

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/Designing Data Intensive Applications The Big Ideas Behind Reliable, Scalable, and Maintainable Systems#^ddia-transactions-simplify-apps]]

## Related

- [[Software complexity comes from dependencies and obscurity]]
- [[RFC-03 - Frontend Error Handeling]]
- [[Technical Vision]]

---
id: Reliable systems tolerate faults instead of pretending faults disappear
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Designing Data-Intensive Applications]]"
---

## Claim

Reliability comes less from eliminating all faults and more from designing systems so local faults do not become user-visible failures.

## Notes

- Faults are unavoidable in real systems, so fault tolerance matters more than fantasies of perfect components.
- A useful distinction is between a component deviating from spec and the whole service failing the user.
- Reliability work is about limiting blast radius and preserving service under imperfect conditions.
- Good system design assumes failure will happen and decides in advance how the rest of the system should respond.

## Implications

- Design for graceful degradation instead of assuming ideal infrastructure.
- Evaluate reliability at the user-facing service boundary, not only at individual components.
- Fault handling is part of architecture, not an afterthought.

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/Designing Data Intensive Applications The Big Ideas Behind Reliable, Scalable, and Maintainable Systems#^ddia-faults-failures]]

## Related

- [[RFC-03 - Frontend Error Handeling]]
- [[Technical Vision]]
- [[Quality is a speed strategy, not a tradeoff]]

---
id: API choices should start from consumers and communication boundaries
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/FE Design System API]]"
---

## Claim

The best API style depends first on who is communicating and what boundary the API must serve.

## Notes

- Useful distinctions include client-to-server versus service-to-service and public versus private APIs.
- The same system may reasonably use different API styles at different boundaries.
- Consumer needs determine whether flexibility, cacheability, type sharing, or performance matters most.
- API design should begin with boundary and ownership questions before protocol preference.

## Implications

- Pick API styles per boundary rather than forcing one approach everywhere.
- Separate public API concerns from internal integration concerns.
- Model contracts around the needs of the consumer and the cost of change.

## Related

- [[RFC-01 - Directed, Bounded Frontend Architecture]]
- [[Technical Vision]]
- [[Frontend scaling has architectural and codebase dimensions]]

---
id: Percentiles reveal performance risk better than averages
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Web Performance]]"
---

## Claim

Average performance hides important user experience problems, while percentiles show how bad the experience is for significant groups of users.

## Notes

- Averages smooth over spikes and tail latency.
- Median, 75th, 95th, and 99th percentiles reveal how the system behaves across different user slices.
- The 75th percentile is especially important because it reflects the majority experience used in major web quality evaluation.
- Tail behavior often matters most when protecting trust and usability.

## Implications

- Report percentiles rather than only averages.
- Investigate the tails when users describe the product as intermittently slow.
- Use percentile goals that align with how experience is judged in the ecosystem.

## Related

- [[Scalability depends on explicit load and performance assumptions]]
- [[Web performance should be measured with field and lab data for different questions]]
- [[Reliable systems tolerate faults instead of pretending faults disappear]]
