content.text:
---
id: Maintainability means being able to change code confidently
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Testing & Code Quality]]"
---

## Claim

A maintainable codebase is one where engineers can change behavior, refactor structure, and update dependencies without overwhelming fear.

## Notes

- Maintainability is not just readability; it is the practical ability to evolve the system safely.
- A healthy codebase lets teams change code and know with confidence that they did not break something important.
- Refactoring should feel normal rather than dangerous.
- Dependency updates are a useful stress test of maintainability because they expose hidden coupling and weak confidence.

## Implications

- Judge quality partly by how safely ordinary changes can be made.
- Treat confidence as an engineering outcome, not only as a feeling.
- Improve maintainability by reducing hidden dependencies and increasing feedback.

## Related

- [[RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing]]
- [[Technical Vision]]
- [[Frontend scaling has architectural and codebase dimensions]]

---
id: Incremental design keeps the cost of change low
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Extreme Programming Explained]]"
---

## Claim

The goal is not to avoid design until later, but to keep redesigning in small steps as experience reveals what the system really needs.

## Notes

- XP is not "design nothing"; it is "design always."
- Some upfront design is needed, but only enough to start well.
- As constraints become real, the design should be brought back into alignment gradually and persistently.
- Eliminating duplication is a strong heuristic because duplicate logic makes one change require many edits.
- Daily attention to design keeps the cost of change from rising catastrophically.

## Implications

- Design should happen in the light of experience, not only in advance of it.
- Refactoring is part of delivery, not separate cleanup.
- Keep design investment proportional to what the system has learned so far.

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/Extreme Programming Explained Embrace Change#^xp-incremental-design]]

## Related

- [[Strategic programming treats design work as an investment]]
- [[Software complexity comes from dependencies and obscurity]]
- [[RFC-01 - Directed, Bounded Frontend Architecture]]

---
id: Test-first development is a design tool
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Extreme Programming Explained]]"
---

## Claim

Writing the test first is useful not only because it verifies behaviour, but because it pressures the design toward clearer interfaces and tighter scope.

## Notes

- A failing test clarifies what should happen before implementation details accumulate.
- If a test is hard to write, the problem is often excessive coupling or weak cohesion in the design.
- Test-first work creates a rhythm: test, code, refactor.
- Tests provide confidence, visible progress, and a concrete expression of intent.
- Test-first thinking separates interface from implementation by making the caller's perspective explicit.

## Implications

- Use test difficulty as design feedback, not just as a testing inconvenience.
- Prefer one failing test at a time so learning from implementation can shape the next step.
- Good tests help both present implementation and future maintenance.

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/Extreme Programming Explained Embrace Change#^xp-test-first]]

## Related

- [[RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing]]
- [[Comments should capture non-obvious design knowledge]]
- [[Software complexity comes from dependencies and obscurity]]

---
id: Well-architected UI applications need patterns processes and systems
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Testing & Code Quality]]"
---

## Claim

Frontend quality comes from a layered system: patterns shape the code, processes shape decisions, and systems enforce feedback.

## Notes

- Useful architectural patterns include abstractions, state management choices, and a common structure for data fetching, business logic, and UI.
- Processes such as code review, blueprints, and design documents help teams make better decisions before entropy accumulates.
- Systems such as testing infrastructure, static analysis, and build automation turn quality expectations into repeatable feedback.
- A well-architected UI application also depends on deployment infrastructure and separation of concerns, not only on tests.

## Heuristic

When quality feels inconsistent, ask whether the missing ingredient is a pattern, a process, or a system.

## Related

- [[RFC-01 - Directed, Bounded Frontend Architecture]]
- [[RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing]]
- [[Technical Vision]]
- [[Nx]]

---
id: Small batches preserve flow and reduce integration risk
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Extreme Programming Explained]]"
---

## Claim

Software delivery becomes more stable when integration, testing, and deployment happen in small batches rather than in large delayed merges.

## Notes

- Integration cost is unpredictable and grows the longer teams wait.
- Fast builds and frequent integration keep feedback close to the change.
- Daily deployment is an extreme direction, but it reveals the value of shrinking the gap between local code and production reality.
- Flow is created by a smooth stream of working software, not by occasional large releases.
- Big changes can still ship incrementally if the user-visible keystone is delayed until the final step.

## Implications

- Shorten build times until running them frequently feels natural.
- Integrate at least every few hours when possible.
- Treat long-lived divergence from production as risk, not normality.

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/Extreme Programming Explained Embrace Change#^xp-small-batches-ci]]

## Related

- [[developer velocity]]
- [[RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing]]
- [[Technical Vision]]

---
id: Cognitive load is a developer experience problem not just a code complexity problem
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Developer Experience]]"
---

## Claim

Cognitive load in developer experience comes from both code complexity and the surrounding working environment.

## Notes

- Cognitive load is the mental effort required to understand, change, and validate a system.
- It grows when code structure is unclear, documentation is weak, architecture is inconsistent, or over-engineering accumulates.
- It also grows through timeline pressure, multitasking, meetings, interruptions, and context switching.
- Useful measures include dependency count, task scope, technical challenge, defect rate, time spent debugging, bug reopen rate, and the effort required to get answers.
- Code-level proxies can include cyclomatic complexity, Halstead volume, and maintainability index.

## Implications

- Reducing cognitive load requires both better code structure and better team operating conditions.
- Documentation, architecture, and testing are DevEx tools as much as quality tools.
- If a team feels overloaded, the answer may be structural, not personal.

## Related

- [[Software complexity comes from dependencies and obscurity]]
- [[Maintainability means being able to change code confidently]]
- [[Testing & Code Quality]]

content.text:
---
id: The Cult of DONE
aliases: 
indexes:
  - "[[Philosophy]]"
---
1. There are three states of being. Not knowing, action and completion.
2. Accept that everything is a draft. It helps to get it done.
3. There is no editing stage.
4. Pretending you know what you are doing is almost the same as knowing what you are doing, so just accept that you know what you are doing even if you dont and do it.
5. Banish procrastination. If you wait more than a week to get and idea done, abandon it.
6. The point of being done is not to finish but to get other things done.
7. Once you are done you can throw it away.
8. Laugh at perfection. Is is boring and keeps you from being done.
9. People without dirty hands are wrong. Doing something makes you right.
10. Failure counts as done. So do mistakes.
11. Destruction is a variant of done.
12. If you have an idea and publish it on the internet, that counts as a ghost of done.
13. done is the engine of more.

content.text:
---
id: Extreme Programming Explained
aliases:
  - XP Explained
  - Extreme Programming Explained
indexes: []
---

## Claim

Extreme Programming treats software development as a learning system: reduce batch size, shorten feedback loops, and keep design adaptable so change stays safe.

## Why this note matters

- XP reframes process as a set of values made concrete through practices.
- Its core insight is not dogma about ceremonies; it is that teams need feedback fast enough to adapt.
- It ties testing, planning, design, integration, deployment, and team health into one operating model.

## Permanent notes from this book

- [[Feedback loops make change survivable]]
- [[Quality is a speed strategy, not a tradeoff]]
- [[Test-first development is a design tool]]
- [[Incremental design keeps the cost of change low]]
- [[Plans are coordination tools, not predictions]]
- [[Small batches preserve flow and reduce integration risk]]

## Connections

- [[Strategic programming treats design work as an investment]]
- [[Software complexity comes from dependencies and obscurity]]
- [[Deep modules hide complexity behind simple interfaces]]
- [[FE Design System Scaling Web Applications]]
- [[RFC-01 - Directed, Bounded Frontend Architecture]]
- [[RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing]]
- [[Technical Vision]]
- [[developer velocity]]

## Practical questions

- Where does feedback arrive too late to be useful?
- Which batch sizes are making integration, testing, or planning harder?
- What quality shortcut is creating slower delivery later?
- Which defects should push us to change the system, not just patch the bug?
- Where should we improve the design today to keep tomorrow's change cheap?

## Source

- Source highlight: [[4 - Source Material/Books/Extreme Programming Explained Embrace Change#^xp-change-paradigm]]

content.text:
Yes — I scanned your `2 - Main Notes`, `3 - Indexes`, some `Work/*`, and a few philosophy drafts/source notes. A few hidden threads stand out:

### 1) **Locality is your deepest design instinct**
Across very different topics, you keep preferring **local reasoning over global entanglement**.

Examples:
- `2 - Main Notes/Formatting contexts make layout rules local and predictable.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/Deep modules hide complexity behind simple interfaces.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/API choices should start from consumers and communication boundaries.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/Transactions simplify applications by moving guarantees into the database.md`
- `Work/Direct/RFCs/Technical Vision.md`

Hidden connection: whether it's CSS, modules, APIs, or databases, you're really asking the same question:

> “How do I make this understandable without needing the whole system in my head?”

---

### 2) **Your real north star is not speed — it’s cheap adaptation**
A lot of the vault looks like “frontend” or “architecture,” but the deeper theme is:

> change is inevitable, so design for safe learning.

That shows up in:
- `2 - Main Notes/Maintainability means being able to change code confidently.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/Incremental design keeps the cost of change low.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/Feedback loops make change survivable.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/Plans are coordination tools, not predictions.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/Small batches preserve flow and reduce integration risk.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/Quality is a speed strategy, not a tradeoff.md`

So the vault is less about “how to build software” and more about **how to stay effective under uncertainty**.

---

### 3) **You consistently reject fake metrics**
In both productivity and performance notes, you distrust simplistic proxies.

Examples:
- `2 - Main Notes/Percentiles reveal performance risk better than averages.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/Web performance should be judged by user perception and business outcomes.md`
- `3 - Indexes/Developer Experience.md`
- `Work/Meiro/developer velocity.md`

Hidden connection:
- In performance: averages are misleading.
- In productivity: output metrics are misleading.
- In testing: coverage alone is misleading.

So there’s a bigger epistemic pattern here:  
you care about **signals that reflect lived reality**, not neat-looking dashboards.

---

### 4) **Boundaries are doing double duty in your thinking**
You don't just use boundaries for organization. You use them for:
- comprehension
- testing
- failure containment
- ownership

Examples:
- `2 - Main Notes/API choices should start from consumers and communication boundaries.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/Reliable systems tolerate faults instead of pretending faults disappear.md`
- `Work/Direct/RFCs/RFC-01 - Directed, Bounded Frontend Architecture.md`
- `Work/Direct/RFCs/RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing.md`
- `Work/Direct/RFCs/RFC-03 - Frontend Error Handeling.md`

Hidden connection:
A boundary in your notes is never just a border. It’s a way to make a system both **legible** and **survivable**.

---

### 5) **There’s a strong unlinked bridge between your philosophy notes and your DevEx notes**
This one is probably the most interesting.

Your note:
- `2 - Main Notes/Flow state depends on protecting uninterrupted focus and reducing reactive work.md`

rhymes very strongly with:
- `1 - Drafts/Active.md`
- `1 - Drafts/Reactive.md`
- `1 - Drafts/Will to Power.md`
- `1 - Drafts/The Great Health.md`
- `4 - Source Material/Books/Nietzsche's System.md`

Hidden connection:
Your engineering notes treat “reactive work” as corrosive to flow.  
Your Nietzsche cluster treats the reactive stance as a lower mode of life.

That suggests a deeper shared theme:

> good systems — and maybe good lives — preserve agency instead of trapping you in reaction.

This connection is semantic, not structural yet. Your graph probably won’t show it clearly.

---

### 6) **You normalize failure in both technical and philosophical notes**
Examples:
- `2 - Main Notes/Feedback loops make change survivable.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/Reliable systems tolerate faults instead of pretending faults disappear.md`
- `Work/Direct/RFCs/Technical Vision.md`
- `2 - Main Notes/The Cult of DONE.md`
- `1 - Drafts/The Great Health.md`

Hidden connection:
You repeatedly frame mistakes, drafts, faults, and uncertainty as **material for growth**, not just defects to eliminate.

That’s unusually consistent across:
- XP/process
- reliability thinking
- DevEx
- personal/philosophical notes

---

## Big meta-pattern

Your vault seems to be converging on one worldview:

> **Protect agency under change by making complexity local, feedback fast, and failure survivable.**

That idea shows up in:
- frontend architecture
- testing
- performance
- distributed systems
- developer experience
- Nietzsche-ish philosophy

## A few links I’d add
If you want stronger graph connections, these seem high-value:

content.text:
---
id: Developer experience can be understood through feedback loops cognitive load and flow state
aliases: []
indexes:
  - "[[3 - Indexes/Developer Experience]]"
---

## Claim

A useful model of developer experience has three core dimensions: feedback loops, cognitive load, and flow state.

## Notes

- Feedback loops describe how quickly engineers learn whether a change is valid.
- Cognitive load describes how much mental effort and system knowledge are required to do the work.
- Flow state describes whether engineers can sustain focus without repeated interruption.
- These dimensions interact: slow feedback increases uncertainty, high cognitive load makes changes harder, and interruptions break flow.

## Implications

- Developer productivity should be improved by shaping the system, not only by pushing people harder.
- Teams can diagnose experience problems by asking which of the three dimensions is weakest.
- A mature productivity model combines perception, workflow metrics, and operating conditions.

## Related

- [[Fast feedback loops are a core dimension of developer experience]]
- [[Cognitive load is a developer experience problem not just a code complexity problem]]
- [[Flow state depends on protecting uninterrupted focus and reducing reactive work]]

arguments.command:
python - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
import re
for p in Path('2 - Main Notes').glob('*.md'):
    text=p.read_text(errors='ignore')
    m=re.search(r'indexes:\n((?:\s+- .*\n)+)', text)
    if m and m.group(1).count('\n')>1:
        print(p.name)
        print(m.group(1))
PY

Drafts may preserve the analysis closely, but they might be less reusable. I could combine both elements by creating a synthesis note with a section for the core claim. arguments.content:
---
id: Hidden connections across my notes
aliases:
  - vault synthesis
indexes:
---

## Core pattern

Across software, performance, and philosophy, the same pattern keeps recurring: protect agency under change by making complexity local, feedback fast, and failure survivable.

## Hidden connections

### 1. Locality is the recurring design instinct

Across very different topics, the same preference shows up: make behavior understandable without needing the whole system in your head.

- In CSS, predictability comes from local layout rules.
- In code design, maintainability comes from hiding complexity behind small interfaces.
- In architecture, boundaries reduce the amount of global knowledge required.
- In data systems, guarantees are most useful when placed at the lowest sensible layer.

Linked notes:
- [[Formatting contexts make layout rules local and predictable]]
- [[Deep modules hide complexity behind simple interfaces]]
- [[API choices should start from consumers and communication boundaries]]
- [[Transactions simplify applications by moving guarantees into the database]]
- [[Technical Vision]]

### 2. The real goal is cheap adaptation, not raw speed

A deeper theme across the software notes is that change is inevitable, so the goal is not static perfection but safe adaptation.

- Fast feedback makes course correction cheaper.
- Small batches reduce risk.
- Incremental design keeps systems aligned with reality.
- Quality preserves speed over time rather than slowing it down.

Linked notes:
- [[Maintainability means being able to change code confidently]]
- [[Incremental design keeps the cost of change low]]
- [[Feedback loops make change survivable]]
- [[Plans are coordination tools, not predictions]]
- [[Small batches preserve flow and reduce integration risk]]
- [[Quality is a speed strategy, not a tradeoff]]

### 3. There is recurring skepticism toward fake metrics

The notes repeatedly reject simplified measurements that hide lived reality.

- In performance, averages hide the tail.
- In productivity, output metrics miss developer experience.
- In testing, numeric coverage can miss behavioural confidence.

The recurring preference is for measures that reflect actual experience, not only convenient dashboards.

Linked notes:
- [[Percentiles reveal performance risk better than averages]]
- [[Web performance should be judged by user perception and business outcomes]]
- [[Web performance should be measured with field and lab data for different questions]]
- [[Developer experience can be understood through feedback loops cognitive load and flow state]]
- [[developer velocity]]

### 4. Boundaries are doing more than organization

Boundaries repeatedly appear as a multi-purpose tool:

- they reduce cognitive load
- they create stable testing surfaces
- they contain failure
- they clarify ownership

A boundary in these notes is not just a border. It is a way to make a system more legible and more survivable.

Linked notes:
- [[API choices should start from consumers and communication boundaries]]
- [[Reliable systems tolerate faults instead of pretending faults disappear]]
- [[RFC-01 - Directed, Bounded Frontend Architecture]]
- [[RFC-02 - Behavior Focused Testing]]
- [[RFC-03 - Frontend Error Handeling]]
- [[Technical Vision]]

### 5. Philosophy and developer experience converge on agency versus reaction

There is a strong conceptual rhyme between the philosophy cluster and the developer-experience cluster.

- In the DevEx notes, reactive work breaks focus and flow.
- In the Nietzsche notes, the active/reactive distinction functions as a deeper value contrast.
- In both cases, the preferred condition is one where a person or system can act from its own structure rather than only respond to interruptions or pressures.

Linked notes:
- [[Flow state depends on protecting uninterrupted focus and reducing reactive work]]
- [[Active]]
- [[Reactive]]
- [[Will to Power]]
- [[The Great Health]]

### 6. Failure is treated as material for learning, not only as something to eliminate

A recurring attitude across technical and philosophical notes is that faults, drafts, and mistakes are not just defects. They are part of the process by which systems improve.

- Reliability work assumes faults will happen and designs for survival.
- XP treats feedback as the path to adaptation.
- Draft-oriented notes treat incompleteness as a productive state.

Linked notes:
- [[Feedback loops make change survivable]]
- [[Reliable systems tolerate faults instead of pretending faults disappear]]
- [[Technical Vision]]
- [[The Cult of DONE]]
- [[The Great Health]]

## Working synthesis

- Good systems preserve agency under change.
- Local reasoning reduces cognitive load.
- Fast feedback reduces uncertainty.
- Strong boundaries reduce blast radius.
- Useful measurement tracks lived experience rather than easy proxies.
- A healthy operating model favors active shaping over reactive firefighting.

## Related

- [[Software complexity comes from dependencies and obscurity]]
- [[Cognitive load is a developer experience problem not just a code complexity problem]]
- [[Fast feedback loops are a core dimension of developer experience]]
- [[Test-first development is a design tool]]
- [[Well-architected UI applications need patterns processes and systems]]
